meta.title: Django Views Tutorial – Function-Based vs Class-Based Views (2026)
meta.description: Learn how to handle HTTP requests in Django. Master Function-Based Views (FBV), Class-Based Views (CBV), and the Request/Response lifecycle.

content.value: In the Django MVT (Model-View-Template) architecture, the **View** is the 'controller' logic. It receives an `HttpRequest`, performs the necessary logic (like fetching data from a database or validating a form), and returns an `HttpResponse`. It acts as the bridge between your data (Models) and your presentation (Templates).
Function-Based Views are the simplest way to write views. They are just Python functions that take a request object as the first argument. They are excellent for simple logic and are highly readable.

As your project grows, you might find yourself repeating the same logic. Class-Based Views allow you to use inheritance and mixins to reuse code. Django provides generic views for common tasks like listing objects or showing details.

from django.views.generic import ListView
from .models import Project

class ProjectListView(ListView):
    model = Project
    template_name = 'projects/list.html'
    context_object_name = 'my_projects'
Every view starts with a request and must end with a response. Django processes the request through middleware before it ever hits your view function.

value: **render()**: Combines a template with a context dictionary and returns an HttpResponse.
**redirect()**: Sends the user to a different URL (301 or 302 status).
**get_object_or_404()**: Tries to get an object from a model; raises a 404 error if not found (cleaner than try/except).

faq.question: What is the 'request' object?
faq.answer: The 'request' object contains metadata about the incoming request, including user session data, POST data (request.POST), and headers.

faq.question: Which should I use: FBVs or CBVs?
faq.answer: Start with Function-Based Views to learn the flow. Switch to Class-Based Views for standard CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete) to save time and reduce code.

faq.question: How do I protect a view so only logged-in users can see it?
faq.answer: For FBVs, use the @login_required decorator. For CBVs, inherit from the LoginRequiredMixin.
